meta.title: SF-SPAP-1: Platform Fundamentals, Loading and Querying

questions.stem: Which Snowflake layer stores table data in compressed columnar files?

options.explanation: Cloud Services handles metadata management, query optimization, and authentication — it does not store table data.
The Storage layer persists all table data as compressed, columnar micro-partition files in cloud object storage.
Virtual warehouses provide compute resources for query execution; they read from Storage but do not persist data themselves.
Marketplace is a discovery and distribution channel for data products, not a storage layer.

questions.stem: What is a virtual warehouse used for in Snowflake?

options.explanation: Micro-partitions are stored in the Storage layer, not inside a warehouse.
Virtual warehouses are clusters of compute resources that execute SQL queries, DML statements, and data loading operations.
Role hierarchy is a security concept managed through Cloud Services and RBAC, not by warehouses.
Publishing listings is a Marketplace/Sharing capability, unrelated to warehouse compute.

questions.stem: Which statement about Snowflake compute and storage is correct?

options.explanation: Storage scales automatically as data grows; resizing warehouses affects compute capacity only.
Snowflake's architecture decouples compute from storage, allowing each to scale independently based on workload and data volume needs.

options.text: Adding users requires a larger warehouse
options.explanation: User count does not determine warehouse size; warehouse sizing is based on query concurrency and complexity.

options.text: Data sharing copies table data into each consumer account
options.explanation: Snowflake's secure data sharing exposes live data by reference — no physical copy is made in the consumer account.

questions.stem: Which object hierarchy is valid in Snowflake?

options.explanation: The Snowflake hierarchy flows from Organization (top level) to Account, then to Database, Schema, and finally Table (or other data objects).
Organizations contain Accounts, not the other way around; warehouses are also not part of the data object hierarchy.
Account is the root logical container under Organization; databases do not contain accounts or warehouses.
Roles are security principals, not structural object containers in the hierarchy.

questions.stem: Which interface is specifically a command-line client for Snowflake?

options.explanation: Snowsight is the browser-based web UI, not a CLI tool.
SnowSQL is Snowflake's dedicated command-line interface (CLI) for connecting to Snowflake and running SQL commands from a terminal.
Snowpipe is a continuous data ingestion service, not a client interface.
Snowpark is a developer API framework for Python, Java, and Scala — not a CLI.

questions.stem: A team needs 90 days of Time Travel for selected tables. Which edition requirement matters most?

options.explanation: Standard Edition supports a maximum Time Travel retention of only 1 day.

options.text: Enterprise Edition or higher is required
options.explanation: Time Travel retention beyond 1 day (up to 90 days) requires Enterprise Edition or higher.
Warehouse size has no effect on Time Travel retention period — it is governed by account edition and the `DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS` parameter.

options.text: Business Critical is the only option
options.explanation: Business Critical is not the only qualifying edition; Enterprise Edition already supports the 90-day maximum.

questions.stem: Which activities are handled by the Cloud Services layer? (Select TWO)

options.explanation: The Cloud Services layer performs query compilation, optimization, and maintains all metadata about databases, schemas, and micro-partitions.

options.text: Persisting table files in object storage
options.explanation: Persisting table files in object storage is the responsibility of the Storage layer, not Cloud Services.
Cloud Services enforces authentication and evaluates role-based access control for every request.

options.text: Running every query operator directly on raw data
options.explanation: Query operators are executed by virtual warehouses (the Compute layer), not by Cloud Services.

questions.stem: What does auto-resume on a warehouse do?

options.text: Drops cached query results after inactivity
options.explanation: Dropping cached results is not related to auto-resume; result cache persistence is governed by separate mechanisms.

options.text: Starts compute automatically when a query needs it
options.explanation: The `AUTO_RESUME` property automatically restarts a suspended warehouse when a SQL statement or query is submitted to it, so users do not need to manually resume it.

options.text: Replicates warehouse settings to other accounts
options.explanation: Auto-resume has no effect on replication — it only controls whether a suspended warehouse starts on demand.

options.text: Converts a single-cluster warehouse into multi-cluster
options.explanation: Transitioning to multi-cluster mode requires changing the `MAX_CLUSTER_COUNT` parameter, not auto-resume.

questions.stem: Which Snowflake object is best for organizing related tables and views within a database?

options.explanation: A Warehouse provides compute resources; it is not part of the data object containment hierarchy.
A Schema is the logical namespace inside a database that groups related tables, views, stages, and other data objects together.
A Role is a security principal used to grant privileges, not a container for data objects.
A Pipe is a Snowpipe object for continuous ingestion; it does not organize tables or views.

questions.stem: Why can two teams run large queries at the same time without one warehouse blocking the other?

options.text: Because all queries run in Cloud Services
options.explanation: Cloud Services handles coordination and metadata, not direct query execution — warehouses do the actual compute work.

options.text: Because each team can use a separate virtual warehouse against shared storage
options.explanation: Snowflake's separation of compute and storage allows multiple independent virtual warehouses to query the same data in Storage concurrently without resource contention.

options.text: Because Snowflake duplicates the database for each user
options.explanation: Snowflake does not duplicate database objects per user; all warehouses read the same shared storage.

options.text: Because every table has its own built-in warehouse
options.explanation: Tables do not have dedicated built-in warehouses; compute resources are provided by separately provisioned virtual warehouses.

questions.stem: Which statement best describes micro-partitions?

options.text: Users must define them manually before loading data
options.explanation: Micro-partitions are created automatically by Snowflake as data is loaded — no manual definition is required.

options.text: They are automatically maintained units of columnar storage used for metadata and pruning
options.explanation: Micro-partitions are contiguous units of storage (50–500 MB of uncompressed data) that Snowflake automatically creates and maintains, storing rich metadata used for partition pruning during queries.

options.text: They are identical to traditional database partitions
options.explanation: Micro-partitions differ from traditional partitions: they are immutable, automatically managed, and use columnar storage with rich metadata rather than user-defined row-based ranges.

options.text: They only exist for temporary tables
options.explanation: Micro-partitions exist for all table types including permanent, transient, and external tables.

questions.stem: Which client experience is Snowflake's web-based user interface?

options.explanation: Snowpark is a developer API framework for Python, Java, and Scala — not a web UI.
Snowsight is Snowflake's browser-based web UI, providing worksheets, dashboards, data exploration, and administration capabilities.
SnowCD is the Snowflake Connectivity Diagnostic tool used to test network connectivity, not a query interface.
Snowpipe Streaming is an SDK-based API for low-latency continuous data ingestion, not a web interface.

questions.stem: Which stage is scoped to a single table by default?

options.explanation: A user stage is scoped to an individual user (`@~`) and can be used to load data into any table that user has access to.
A named internal stage is a separately created object with its own name and can be shared across multiple tables and operations.
A table stage is automatically created with every table (referenced as `@%<table_name>`) and is scoped exclusively to that table.
An external stage points to a customer-managed cloud storage location such as S3, Azure Blob, or GCS and is not scoped to a single table.

questions.stem: Which command is used for bulk loading files into a table?

options.explanation: `MERGE INTO` performs upsert DML operations on existing table rows — it does not load staged files.
`COPY INTO <table>` is the primary command for bulk loading staged files into a Snowflake table.
`PUT` uploads files from a local machine to an internal stage, which is a staging step before loading — it does not load data into a table.
`GET` downloads files from an internal stage to the local filesystem — it is the reverse of PUT and does not load tables.

questions.stem: What is a primary benefit of Snowpipe compared with scheduled bulk COPY jobs?

options.explanation: Snowpipe still requires a stage to be defined — the pipe object references the stage and a COPY INTO statement.

Snowpipe is designed for continuous, near-real-time ingestion triggered by file arrival events (cloud storage notifications or REST API calls), unlike scheduled batch COPY jobs that run at fixed intervals.
Snowpipe supports all file formats that `COPY INTO` supports, including CSV, Parquet, JSON, Avro, and others.
Snowpipe handles ingestion only; transformations still require virtual warehouses or Snowpark.

questions.stem: Which file formats can Snowflake load natively without converting files to CSV first? (Select TWO)

options.explanation: Snowflake natively supports loading Parquet files, including directly into a VARIANT column or with schema detection.
JSON is a first-class semi-structured format that Snowflake can ingest natively into VARIANT columns.
Executable binaries are not a supported data format for loading into Snowflake tables.
PowerPoint slides are presentation documents, not a supported data ingestion format in Snowflake.

questions.stem: A team wants a table with no Fail-safe and lower recovery cost for transient staging data. Which table type fits best?

options.explanation: Permanent tables include Fail-safe (7 days for Enterprise and above) and support up to 90 days of Time Travel — both of which incur additional storage costs.
Transient tables have a maximum Time Travel retention of 1 day and no Fail-safe period, making them cost-effective for staging data that does not need extended recovery protection.
External tables reference data in an external stage and do not support Time Travel or Fail-safe; they are used for querying data that lives outside Snowflake, not for staging.
Hybrid Table is a separate table type optimized for transactional (HTAP) workloads, not for cost-saving on transient staging data.

questions.stem: Which COPY option controls behavior when a few records in a file are malformed?

options.explanation: The `ON_ERROR` option in `COPY INTO` specifies how Snowflake should handle errors — for example `CONTINUE` to skip bad rows, `SKIP_FILE` to bypass files with errors, or `ABORT_STATEMENT` (the default) to fail the entire load.
`AUTO_RESUME` is a warehouse property that automatically restarts a suspended warehouse, not a COPY INTO error-handling option.
`WAREHOUSE_SIZE` is a warehouse property for sizing compute clusters, not a COPY option related to data errors.
`PURGE` controls whether successfully loaded files are deleted from the stage after loading — it does not govern error handling for malformed records.

questions.stem: What does the PUT command do in Snowflake?

options.text: Uploads local files to an internal stage
options.explanation: `PUT` copies one or more files from the local machine (or a local path) to a Snowflake internal stage, compressing and encrypting them in transit.
Creating an external stage integration requires `CREATE STORAGE INTEGRATION` and `CREATE STAGE` DDL statements — not `PUT`.
Moving data between tables uses DML commands like `INSERT INTO ... SELECT` or `COPY INTO <table>` from another table — not `PUT`.
Snowpipe notifications are triggered by cloud event notifications (such as S3 SQS) or REST API calls — the `PUT` command has no role in triggering pipes.

questions.stem: Which data type is commonly used to store semi-structured JSON in Snowflake?

options.explanation: `OBJECT_ID` is not a Snowflake data type; there is no such built-in type for semi-structured storage.
The VARIANT data type can store any semi-structured value — JSON, Avro, ORC, XML, or Parquet — up to 16 MB per value, and supports dot/colon notation for querying nested elements.
GEOGRAPHY is a specialized geospatial data type for storing and querying geographic locations, not JSON documents.
VECTOR is a data type for storing embedding arrays used in similarity search, not for general semi-structured JSON content.

questions.stem: Why might a team choose an external stage instead of an internal stage?

options.text: To reference files in cloud storage they already manage
options.explanation: An external stage points to a customer-managed cloud storage location such as Amazon S3, Azure Blob Storage, or Google Cloud Storage, allowing teams to load data directly from storage they already control without duplicating files.
External stages typically require credentials or a storage integration to authenticate access to the cloud storage location — they do not eliminate credential management.

options.text: Because COPY INTO cannot read internal stages
options.explanation: `COPY INTO` works with both internal and external stages — internal stages are a fully supported source.

options.text: Because external stages are required for temporary tables
options.explanation: Temporary tables have no dependency on external stages; they can be loaded from internal or external stages.

questions.stem: Which statement about temporary tables is correct?

options.text: They are visible to all users in an account
options.explanation: Temporary tables are private to the creating session — other users cannot see or query them.

options.text: They persist until manually dropped across sessions
options.explanation: Temporary tables are automatically purged at session end; they cannot persist across sessions.

options.text: They exist only for the duration of the session that created them
options.explanation: Temporary tables are session-scoped: they are automatically dropped when the session ends and are visible only to the session that created them.
Temporary tables have no Fail-safe period, making them unsuitable for data that requires recovery protection.

questions.stem: What does setting PURGE = TRUE in a COPY INTO statement do?

options.text: Removes successfully loaded files from the stage after loading
options.explanation: `PURGE = TRUE` instructs Snowflake to delete files from the stage after they are successfully loaded, helping to keep the stage clean and avoid accumulating old files.

options.text: Deletes existing rows from the target table before loading
options.explanation: PURGE only affects stage files — it does not truncate or delete rows from the target table before loading.

options.text: Disables Time Travel for the target table
options.explanation: Time Travel is controlled by the `DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS` parameter on the table, not by COPY INTO options.

options.text: Skips files that were already loaded previously
options.explanation: Tracking previously loaded files is handled by Snowflake's load history metadata (used by the default `LOAD_UNCERTAIN_FILES` behavior), not by the PURGE option.

questions.stem: Which clause filters grouped results after aggregation?

options.explanation: `WHERE` filters individual rows before grouping and aggregation occurs, so aggregate functions cannot be used in a WHERE clause.
`HAVING` filters groups produced by `GROUP BY` after aggregation is computed — for example, `HAVING COUNT(*) > 5` keeps only groups with more than five rows.
`QUALIFY` filters the result of window functions (after they are computed), not aggregate groups.
`LIMIT` restricts the number of rows returned in the final result set — it does not filter based on aggregate conditions.

questions.stem: Which join returns only rows with matching keys in both tables?

options.explanation: A `LEFT OUTER JOIN` preserves all rows from the left table, even when there is no matching row in the right table (right-side columns are NULL for unmatched rows).
A `FULL OUTER JOIN` returns all rows from both tables, filling NULLs for the side that has no match.
An `INNER JOIN` returns only rows where the join condition is satisfied in both tables — unmatched rows from either side are excluded.
A `CROSS JOIN` produces a Cartesian product of both tables with no join condition — every row from the left is paired with every row from the right.

questions.stem: How do you access the `city` field inside a VARIANT column named `payload`?

options.explanation: Dot notation (`payload.city`) would be interpreted as a table-alias.column reference rather than a VARIANT path traversal.
Snowflake uses colon notation (`payload:city`) to traverse top-level keys in a VARIANT column. Bracket notation (`payload['city']`) is also valid.
`city(payload)` is not valid SQL syntax for extracting a field from a semi-structured value.
`->` is JSON extraction operator syntax from other databases (e.g., MySQL, PostgreSQL) and is not supported in Snowflake SQL.

questions.stem: Which functions are aggregate functions in SQL? (Select TWO)

options.explanation: `COUNT` is an aggregate function that counts the number of rows (or non-NULL values) across a group.
`SUM` is an aggregate function that adds up numeric values across all rows in a group.
`COALESCE` is a scalar function that returns the first non-NULL value from a list of expressions — it operates on individual rows, not across groups.
`SUBSTR` (or `SUBSTRING`) is a scalar string function that extracts a portion of a string value — it is not an aggregate function.

questions.stem: Which clause is most appropriate for sorting query output from highest revenue to lowest revenue?

options.explanation: `GROUP BY` defines grouping keys for aggregation and does not sort output — `DESC` is not valid syntax as part of a GROUP BY clause.
`ORDER BY revenue DESC` sorts the final result set by the revenue column in descending order (largest value first).
`HAVING` filters groups based on aggregate conditions; it is not used for sorting and does not accept sort direction.
`QUALIFY` filters rows based on window function results and does not sort the result set.

questions.stem: Which result is produced by a LEFT JOIN when a left-side row has no match on the right?

options.text: The row is removed from the result set
options.explanation: Removing unmatched rows is the behavior of an INNER JOIN, not a LEFT JOIN.

options.text: The row is returned with NULLs for right-side columns
options.explanation: A `LEFT JOIN` (LEFT OUTER JOIN) preserves every row from the left table; for rows with no matching row on the right, all right-side column values appear as NULL in the result.

options.text: The query fails with an error
options.explanation: Having no match on the right side is a normal, expected outcome for a LEFT JOIN — it does not cause an error.

options.text: The row is duplicated once per warehouse cluster
options.explanation: Row duplication related to warehouse clusters is not a concept in Snowflake SQL behavior.

questions.stem: Which expression is best for returning only the date portion of a timestamp column?

options.explanation: `TO_DATE` converts or casts a timestamp value to a DATE, stripping the time component and returning only the year-month-day portion.
`OBJECT_CONSTRUCT` builds a VARIANT object from key-value pairs — it has no date extraction purpose.
`PARSE_JSON` converts a JSON string into a VARIANT value — it cannot be applied to a timestamp to extract a date.
`BASE64_ENCODE` encodes a binary or string value to Base64 format — it is unrelated to date extraction from timestamps.

questions.stem: A query groups sales data by region and calculates AVG(sales). Which clause determines the grouping key?

options.explanation: `GROUP BY region` partitions all rows into groups by their region value, and the aggregate `AVG(sales)` is then computed independently for each group.
`ORDER BY region` sorts the output rows — it does not create groups for aggregation.
`LIMIT` restricts the number of rows returned — `LIMIT region` is invalid SQL syntax and has nothing to do with grouping.
`FROM region` would reference a table named `region`, not define a grouping key.

questions.stem: Which function converts a string containing JSON into a semi-structured VARIANT value?

options.explanation: `PARSE_JSON` takes a JSON-formatted string and returns a VARIANT value, enabling colon-path querying and semi-structured operations.
`TO_GEOGRAPHY` converts a WKT, WKB, or GeoJSON string into a Snowflake GEOGRAPHY value — not a general VARIANT.
`FLATTEN` is a table function that laterally explodes arrays or objects inside a VARIANT into multiple rows; it does not parse a raw JSON string.
`SYSTEM$WAIT` is a system function that pauses execution for a specified number of seconds — it has no JSON parsing capability.

questions.stem: What does FLATTEN help do with semi-structured data?

options.explanation: Encryption at rest is handled automatically by Snowflake for all data; FLATTEN has no encryption role.

options.text: Expand arrays or nested objects into individual rows
options.explanation: `FLATTEN` is a table function that laterally joins a VARIANT column's array or object elements as individual rows, making it possible to query nested data in a relational style.

options.text: Automatically resize warehouses for JSON queries
options.explanation: Warehouse resizing is controlled by the `ALTER WAREHOUSE` command or auto-scaling settings, not by FLATTEN.

options.text: Convert SQL queries into Python code
options.explanation: FLATTEN is a SQL table function — it does not translate SQL to Python.

questions.stem: Which clause limits a result set to the first 10 rows returned?

options.explanation: `TOP 10 ONLY` is not valid Snowflake SQL syntax; Snowflake does not support the `SELECT TOP N` form.
`LIMIT 10` is standard SQL syntax (also supported as `FETCH FIRST 10 ROWS ONLY`) that restricts the number of rows in the result set to 10.
`ROWS 10` is not a valid Snowflake SQL clause for row limiting.
`HAVING COUNT(*) <= 10` filters aggregate groups where the count is 10 or fewer — it does not limit the number of rows in the output.

questions.stem: When converting a timestamp between time zones, which Snowflake data concern is most relevant?

options.explanation: Warehouse billing is based on compute time, not on timezone conversions within SQL.

options.text: Timestamp type and timezone handling functions
options.explanation: Snowflake has three timestamp types — `TIMESTAMP_NTZ` (no timezone), `TIMESTAMP_LTZ` (local timezone), and `TIMESTAMP_TZ` (with offset stored) — and functions like `CONVERT_TIMEZONE` handle conversion between zones correctly depending on which type is used.
Marketplace listing regions are a distribution concern, unrelated to timestamp type handling in queries.
Micro-partition file size relates to storage optimization and query pruning, not to timezone conversion logic.

questions.stem: Which Snowflake security model is used to grant privileges to users through roles?

options.explanation: While Snowflake supports discretionary access control (DAC) alongside RBAC, it does not use Attribute-Based Access Control (ABAC) as its primary model.
Snowflake uses Role-Based Access Control (RBAC): privileges are granted to roles, and roles are then granted to users, so users inherit the access rights of their active role.
MAC address filtering is a network-layer concept; Snowflake's security model operates at the role and privilege level, not at the network hardware level.
"row hash security" is not a Snowflake concept; row-level security in Snowflake is implemented via row access policies, which are a distinct feature.

questions.stem: Which system role is primarily responsible for managing grants, users, and roles in Snowflake?

options.explanation: PUBLIC is a default role automatically granted to all users with minimal privileges; it is not an administrative role.
SECURITYADMIN has the ability to manage all users, roles, and grants across the account — it is the primary role for security administration.
SYSADMIN focuses on creating and managing databases, schemas, and warehouses — it handles object administration rather than security grants.
USERADMIN can manage users and roles but has a narrower scope than SECURITYADMIN, which can also manage grants on all objects.

questions.stem: What does MFA (multi-factor authentication) primarily add to username/password authentication?

options.text: A second verification factor beyond the password
options.explanation: MFA adds a second factor — typically a time-based one-time passcode (TOTP) from an authenticator app like Duo — that must be verified in addition to the password, significantly reducing the risk of credential-only attacks.
Automatic warehouse scaling is controlled by multi-cluster warehouse settings (`MAX_CLUSTER_COUNT`), which are entirely unrelated to MFA.
Snowflake encrypts all data in transit using TLS by default for all editions, independent of whether MFA is enabled.
Secure data sharing is a separate Snowflake capability that has no connection to authentication methods like MFA.

questions.stem: Which statement about network policies is correct?

options.text: They control SQL syntax allowed in worksheets
options.explanation: Network policies operate at the network connection level — they do not control SQL syntax or worksheet behavior.

options.text: They can restrict which client IP addresses are allowed to connect
options.explanation: Snowflake network policies define allowed (and optionally blocked) IP address ranges, restricting which clients can connect to an account or specific users.
Network policies are a network access control mechanism and work alongside RBAC, not as a replacement for role-based grants.

options.text: They apply only to external stage connections
options.explanation: Network policies apply to all Snowflake connections (Snowsight, SnowSQL, JDBC, etc.) — they are not limited to external stage operations.

questions.stem: How is data encrypted when moving between a client and Snowflake?

options.explanation: Snowflake enforces TLS (Transport Layer Security) encryption for all data in transit between clients and the Snowflake service, on all editions by default.
Customer-managed keys (Tri-Secret Secure) address encryption at rest and key custody — not general in-transit encryption, which is TLS-based and always active.

options.text: It is not encrypted unless Business Critical is enabled
options.explanation: TLS encryption in transit is enforced on all Snowflake editions, not only Business Critical.
Local disk cache refers to client-side storage, which is unrelated to transit encryption between the client and the Snowflake service.

questions.stem: Which features provide fine-grained protection of sensitive data values within Snowflake? (Select TWO)

options.explanation: Dynamic data masking applies masking policies to columns, obfuscating or replacing sensitive values (such as PII) for users without the appropriate role.
Row access policies filter which rows a user can see in a query result, enabling row-level security based on the querying user's role or attributes.
Warehouse auto-suspend is a cost control feature that suspends idle compute — it has no data protection or access control function.
Resource monitors track and limit credit consumption — they are a cost governance tool, not a data security feature.

questions.stem: Why is it usually better to grant privileges to a custom role instead of directly to many individual users?

options.text: It increases storage compression for those users
options.explanation: Storage compression is a physical storage optimization independent of how privileges are structured.

options.text: It simplifies administration and supports least-privilege design
options.explanation: Granting privileges to a role and then granting that role to users allows administrators to manage access in one place; adding or removing a user from the role instantly adjusts their access, supporting least-privilege and reducing administrative overhead.

options.text: It is required in order to load JSON data
options.explanation: Loading JSON data is controlled by stage and table privileges — no requirement exists to use custom roles specifically for JSON loading.

options.text: It disables inherited privileges from parent roles
options.explanation: Granting a role to a user causes the user to inherit all privileges of that role; it does not disable privilege inheritance.

questions.stem: Which authentication method is commonly used for service accounts and automation scripts that should avoid storing passwords?

options.explanation: Key-pair authentication uses an RSA public/private key pair — the private key is stored securely on the client and never transmitted, making it well-suited for service accounts and CI/CD pipelines that need to avoid password storage.
"temporary table tokens" is not a Snowflake authentication mechanism.
The Search Optimization Service is a query performance feature for point-lookup queries — it has no role in authentication.
"data exchange credentials" is not a Snowflake authentication mechanism; Data Exchange is a sharing platform.

questions.stem: What is the PUBLIC role in Snowflake?

options.text: A built-in role automatically granted to all users
options.explanation: PUBLIC is a system-defined role that is automatically granted to every user in a Snowflake account, so any privileges granted to PUBLIC are accessible by all users — administrators should be cautious when granting anything to this role.

options.text: The highest administrative role in the account
options.explanation: ACCOUNTADMIN is the highest administrative role; PUBLIC has essentially no elevated privileges by default.

options.text: A network policy object for IP filtering
options.explanation: Network policies are separate security objects that control IP-based access; they are not roles.

options.text: A special type of virtual warehouse
options.explanation: PUBLIC is a security role, not a warehouse type.

questions.stem: Which Snowflake capability lets a provider share live data without copying table files into the consumer account?

options.explanation: Snowflake's secure data sharing allows a provider to expose live database objects (tables, views, secure views) to consumer accounts by reference — no physical data is copied or moved.
Fail-safe is a storage durability feature that allows Snowflake to recover data after extreme events; it is not related to sharing data with other accounts.
Warehouse replication copies warehouse configuration between accounts for disaster recovery or business continuity purposes, not for sharing data access.
PUT and GET are file transfer commands for moving files to and from internal stages — they are not a sharing mechanism.

questions.stem: When is a reader account most useful?

options.text: When the consumer already has its own Snowflake account
options.explanation: If the consumer already has a Snowflake account, the provider can share directly with that account using standard secure data sharing — no reader account is needed.

options.text: When a consumer needs shared data but does not have a Snowflake account
options.explanation: A reader account is a managed account created by a provider specifically for consumers who do not have their own Snowflake account, enabling them to query shared data using compute paid for by the provider.

options.text: When publishing notebooks for internal teams
options.explanation: Publishing notebooks for internal teams uses Snowsight or similar internal tooling, not reader accounts.

options.text: When creating a transient staging table
options.explanation: Transient tables are a local table type in the provider's account; they are unrelated to reader account use cases.

questions.stem: What is the Snowflake Marketplace used for?

options.text: Purchasing warehouse compute credits from other customers
options.explanation: Compute credits are billed directly through Snowflake pricing agreements; there is no peer-to-peer credit marketplace.

options.text: Discovering and consuming third-party data products and applications
options.explanation: Snowflake Marketplace is a catalog where data providers publish listings — including datasets, data applications, and native apps — that consumers can discover and access directly in their Snowflake account.
External stages are configured via DDL (`CREATE STAGE`) and storage integrations — the Marketplace has no role in this.
Encryption key rotation is managed through Snowflake's key management or Tri-Secret Secure, not through the Marketplace.

questions.stem: Which object is created by a provider to expose databases, schemas, and secure views to consumers?

options.explanation: A Share is a named Snowflake object (`CREATE SHARE`) into which the provider grants access to databases, schemas, tables, and secure views; the share is then granted to consumer accounts.
A Pipe is a Snowpipe object for automated data ingestion — it is not a data sharing mechanism.
A masking policy is a column-level security feature that obscures data values — it is a complement to sharing but is not itself the sharing object.
A task graph is a directed acyclic graph of Tasks used for workflow orchestration — it has no role in data sharing.

questions.stem: What is one key difference between a Data Exchange and the Snowflake Marketplace?

options.text: Data Exchange is for private collaboration groups, while Marketplace is a broader public discovery channel
options.explanation: A Data Exchange is a private, invitation-only hub for a defined community of providers and consumers, while the Snowflake Marketplace is an open discovery platform accessible to all Snowflake customers.

options.text: Marketplace requires no Snowflake account to consume listings
options.explanation: Both Marketplace and Data Exchange require a Snowflake account to consume shared data.

options.text: Data Exchange can only share CSV files
options.explanation: Data Exchange shares live database objects (tables, secure views) — not raw files in a specific format like CSV.

options.text: Marketplace listings cannot include native applications
options.explanation: Native applications built with the Native App Framework can be distributed through the Snowflake Marketplace.

questions.stem: Which objects are commonly shared instead of raw base tables to enforce governed access in a share? (Select TWO)

options.explanation: Secure views allow providers to expose only specific columns or filtered rows without revealing the underlying table structure or implementation, enforcing governance over what consumers can see.
Virtual warehouses provide compute resources and cannot be shared as part of a data share object — consumers use their own compute.
Resource monitors are cost-control objects in an account and are not shareable data objects.
Secure UDFs (user-defined functions) can be shared as callable functions in a share, enabling providers to expose computation logic while hiding implementation details.

questions.stem: A provider wants visibility into how often their Marketplace listing is being used by consumers. Which Snowflake feature is most relevant?

options.explanation: The Snowflake Marketplace provides provider analytics dashboards that show listing installs, consumer query activity, and usage trends, helping providers understand adoption.
Micro-partition pruning statistics relate to query performance optimization, not Marketplace listing usage.
Time Travel provides access to historical data states within an account — it does not report on Marketplace listing consumption.
Warehouse auto-resume history tracks when suspended warehouses were restarted — it is unrelated to Marketplace analytics.

questions.stem: Which term best describes granting another account access to selected objects while the provider retains ownership?

options.explanation: Database replication creates a physical copy of a database in another account or region for disaster recovery or business continuity — it transfers a copy, not just access.
Secure data sharing grants a consumer account read access to the provider's database objects by reference — the provider retains full ownership and no data is duplicated.
Data unloading (using `COPY INTO <location>`) exports data to files in a stage or cloud storage — the consumer receives files, not live access to the source objects.
"session cloning" is not a Snowflake concept.

questions.stem: Why are secure views often preferred over base tables in shared datasets?

options.text: They let providers hide underlying table logic and restrict exposed columns or rows
options.explanation: Secure views prevent consumers from inspecting the view definition (unlike standard views), allowing providers to expose only specific columns or filtered rows while concealing the underlying table structure and business logic.

options.text: They automatically resize the provider's warehouse
options.explanation: Warehouse sizing is an independent configuration concern — secure views have no effect on compute resource allocation.

options.text: They eliminate the need for any privilege grants
options.explanation: Secure views still require proper privilege grants; the `SECURE` keyword protects the view definition, not the need for access control.

options.text: They physically copy source rows into the consumer account
options.explanation: Secure data sharing never copies data — consumers query the provider's live storage by reference.

questions.stem: What is Snowpark primarily used for?

options.text: Building DataFrame-style applications in Python, Java, or Scala that run inside Snowflake
options.explanation: Snowpark is Snowflake's developer framework that allows data engineers and scientists to write DataFrame-based and procedural logic in Python, Java, or Scala that is pushed down and executed inside Snowflake.

options.text: Uploading local files to internal stages
options.explanation: Uploading local files to internal stages is done with the `PUT` command via SnowSQL or other clients — not Snowpark.

options.text: Replacing all SQL queries in Snowsight worksheets
options.explanation: Snowpark is an API for programmatic application development; Snowsight worksheets remain the interactive SQL interface and are not replaced by Snowpark.
MFA enrollment is managed through user account settings or SCIM provisioning — not through Snowpark.

questions.stem: Which Snowflake capability lets developers build and host Python-based interactive web apps directly in the platform?

options.explanation: Snowpipe Streaming is an SDK-based API for low-latency row-by-row data ingestion — it is an ingestion service, not an app hosting platform.
Streamlit in Snowflake allows developers to build, host, and share interactive Python-based web applications natively within their Snowflake account, with direct access to Snowflake data.
Tri-Secret Secure is a Business Critical feature for customer-managed encryption key custody — it is unrelated to app development.
Failover groups are used for business continuity and disaster recovery replication between accounts — not for hosting applications.

questions.stem: What is the Snowflake Native App Framework designed for?

options.text: Creating and distributing applications that run in consumer Snowflake accounts
options.explanation: The Native App Framework allows developers to build, package, and distribute Snowflake applications — including logic, data, and UIs — that consumers can install directly into their own Snowflake accounts from the Marketplace or private listings.

options.text: Scheduling automatic warehouse suspension at set times
options.explanation: Automatic warehouse suspension is controlled by the `AUTO_SUSPEND` property and resource monitors, not by the Native App Framework.

options.text: Encrypting external stages with custom TLS certificates
options.explanation: External stage encryption uses storage integrations and cloud provider IAM mechanisms — the Native App Framework has no role in TLS certificate management for stages.

options.text: Replacing role hierarchy with application-defined roles only
options.explanation: The Native App Framework uses application roles for privilege management within an app, but it does not replace the account-level role hierarchy.

questions.stem: Which tool is most directly associated with command-line automation of Snowflake resources and native app packaging?

options.explanation: Snowflake CLI (`snow`) is the modern command-line tool for managing Snowflake objects, running SQL, and packaging and deploying Snowpark functions, Streamlit apps, and Native App Framework applications.
"Marketplace Studio" is not a distinct Snowflake CLI tool; listing management is done via Snowsight's Provider Studio UI or the Snowflake CLI.
Reader accounts are a sharing concept for providing non-Snowflake customers access to shared data — they are not an automation or packaging tool.
The query result cache is a performance feature that automatically stores query results for reuse — it is not a CLI or automation tool.

questions.stem: Which tools are examples of partner or adjacent technologies commonly integrated with Snowflake for data transformation and visualization? (Select TWO)

options.explanation: Dbt (data build tool) is a widely used open-source SQL transformation framework that integrates directly with Snowflake as a partner tool for building data models in the warehouse.
Tableau is a leading BI and data visualization tool that connects to Snowflake through a native connector and is listed as a Technology Partner.
Micro-partitions are an internal Snowflake storage concept, not a partner or external technology integration.
Snowsight worksheets are a native Snowflake UI feature — not a partner or adjacent external tool.

questions.stem: Why might a team choose Streamlit in Snowflake instead of hosting a separate dashboard server externally?

options.text: To keep the app co-located with Snowflake data and eliminate a separate hosting infrastructure
options.explanation: Hosting the Streamlit app inside Snowflake eliminates the need for a separate server or cloud hosting service, keeps the application close to governed data, and simplifies deployment and access management.

options.text: Because Streamlit in Snowflake completely replaces SQL queries
options.explanation: Streamlit in Snowflake apps still use SQL (and Python) to query Snowflake data — Streamlit does not replace SQL.

options.text: Because it requires no Python code to build apps
options.explanation: Streamlit apps require Python code; Streamlit is a Python library for building interactive web UIs.

options.text: Because it only works for external data consumers outside the account
options.explanation: Streamlit in Snowflake apps are primarily designed for internal users within the Snowflake account, though they can be shared selectively.

questions.stem: Which statement best distinguishes Snowpark from standard SQL worksheets in Snowsight?

options.text: Snowpark is for programmatic DataFrame and procedural development, while worksheets provide an interactive SQL environment
options.explanation: Snowpark is a developer API that allows data engineers and scientists to write programmatic DataFrame logic and stored procedures in Python, Java, or Scala; Snowsight worksheets provide an interactive browser-based environment for writing and running SQL.

options.text: Snowpark is a security feature for encrypting worksheets
options.explanation: Snowpark is not a security feature — it is a developer framework for writing code that runs in Snowflake.

options.text: Worksheets are required in order to load staged data files into tables
options.explanation: Staged data can be loaded using `COPY INTO` from any Snowflake client (SnowSQL, JDBC, Snowsight, or Snowpark) — worksheets are not required.

options.text: Snowpark cannot call any built-in SQL functions
options.explanation: Snowpark can call all built-in SQL functions via the `functions` module and supports SQL expression interoperability.

questions.stem: Which TWO properties of Snowpipe distinguish it from a scheduled COPY INTO batch job? (Select TWO)

options.text: It triggers automatically on file arrival via event notifications or REST API
options.explanation: Snowpipe responds to cloud storage event notifications (e.g., S3 SQS/SNS, Azure Event Grid) or REST API calls to process new files as they arrive, enabling near-real-time continuous ingestion.

options.text: It is billed using serverless compute credits rather than warehouse credits
options.explanation: Snowpipe uses Snowflake-managed serverless compute (billed by data volume and processing time at serverless rates) rather than requiring the customer to provision a running virtual warehouse.

options.text: It requires a running virtual warehouse to process each file
options.explanation: One of Snowpipe's key advantages is that it does not require a customer-managed virtual warehouse — it uses serverless compute provisioned by Snowflake.

options.text: It only supports loading into external tables
options.explanation: Snowpipe loads data into regular Snowflake tables, not external tables; external tables reference data in external storage and are not loaded by Snowpipe.

questions.stem: Which TWO clauses can filter rows in a SELECT statement — one before aggregation and one after? (Select TWO)

options.explanation: `WHERE` filters individual rows before grouping and aggregation — it cannot reference aggregate functions.
`HAVING` filters groups after aggregation is computed, and it can reference aggregate expressions like `HAVING COUNT(*) > 5`.
`LIMIT` restricts the number of rows in the final output — it does not filter rows based on data conditions either before or after aggregation.
`ORDER BY` sorts the result set — it does not filter rows at any stage of query execution.

questions.stem: Which TWO system-defined roles in Snowflake are focused on security and privilege management rather than object creation? (Select TWO)

options.explanation: SECURITYADMIN is designed to manage users, roles, and grants across the account — its primary purpose is security administration, not creating databases or warehouses.
USERADMIN is specifically designed to create and manage users and roles — it focuses on identity management rather than data object administration.
SYSADMIN is the system role focused on creating and managing databases, schemas, warehouses, and other data objects — it is an object administration role.
PUBLIC is a default role automatically assigned to all users with essentially no elevated privileges — it is not a security administration role.

questions.stem: Which TWO features help reduce virtual warehouse compute costs when workloads are not running? (Select TWO)

options.explanation: The `AUTO_SUSPEND` property automatically suspends a warehouse after a configured period of inactivity, stopping credit consumption when no queries are running.
Resource monitors allow administrators to set credit quotas and trigger actions (including suspending warehouses) when thresholds are reached, directly controlling compute spend.
Secure data sharing is a data access and distribution feature — it does not affect warehouse compute costs.
Micro-partition pruning is a query performance optimization that reduces the data scanned — it improves query efficiency but does not control warehouse suspension or credit consumption directly.

questions.stem: Which TWO statements accurately describe how secure data sharing works in Snowflake? (Select TWO)

options.text: The consumer queries live provider data without any physical copy being made
options.explanation: Snowflake's secure data sharing works by reference — the consumer account gets read access to the provider's live data objects without any ETL or physical data movement.

options.text: Consumer accounts use their own compute to query shared data
options.explanation: The consumer's virtual warehouse (or reader account compute) executes queries against the provider's shared data, so the consumer's compute costs apply rather than the provider's.

options.text: Sharing always requires the consumer to pay the provider for storage costs
options.explanation: There is no mandatory provider-to-consumer storage billing in standard secure data sharing — the provider pays for their storage and the consumer pays for their own query compute.

options.text: Shared data is exported as CSV files to the consumer account
options.explanation: Secure data sharing never exports or sends CSV files; consumers query the provider's live data directly via Snowflake's storage layer.
